turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I thought every shared state in Rust required a Mutex or RwLock, but the tutorial just showed me using an Actor pattern with mpsc channels and it seems to handle everything without any locking primitives at all.

That's a great observation. The core idea is that in this architecture, each actor owns its own data. When an actor needs to send information out, it uses a channel like tokio::sync::mpsc or std::sync::mpsc, and the receiver side simply consumes messages without ever needing to access the sender's internal state directly.
But what if two different actors try to update a shared counter at the same time? Doesn't that still require some kind of synchronization mechanism like tokio::sync::Mutex?

Exactly. If you need to modify shared mutable state from multiple threads or async tasks, you absolutely should use something like tokio::sync::Mutex or std::sync::Mutex depending on whether you're in an async context or blocking code.
So the tutorial is avoiding these primitives entirely? Is it safe to just pass data through channels and never touch shared memory?

The pattern works because the actors don't share mutable state directly. Instead, they communicate via message passing using types like tokio::sync::mpsc channel or std::sync::mpsc. The sender puts a message into the queue and the receiver takes it out—there's no concurrent write to shared memory.
Wait, so if I want to pass complex objects like Vec<i32> or HashMap<String, bool> through these channels, won't that cause data races?

No. As long as the receiver only reads from the channel and never mutates the object it received directly, there's no race condition. The ownership semantics of Rust ensure that once an actor takes a value out of a channel, it owns that value until it drops or passes it along.
Okay I think I finally get it. But what about watch channels? When do I use those instead of mpsc?

Watch channels are useful when you want to observe state changes without explicitly sending messages every time something updates. They're part of the async ecosystem and work well with tokio's runtime, but they still follow the same principle: no direct shared mutation.

I'm getting this error: cannot borrow mut in mutable ref because it is also borrowed as immutable ref. I tried using std::sync::Mutex inside my tokio runtime and got this panic when trying to write to a shared counter from multiple tasks.

That error happens because you are mixing synchronous mutexes with async code without care. std::sync::Mutex doesn't play well with tokio's runtime unless you wrap it in a special adapter, and even then you can't easily use it inside async blocks directly. The better approach is to avoid blocking the thread entirely by using channels or watch streams instead of holding a lock.
I thought I could just use tokio::sync::Mutex and be done with it like normal async code. Can't I just wrap my counter in that and forget about threads?

You can absolutely use tokio::sync::Mutex, but the key is understanding how it interacts with your runtime. It's designed to work seamlessly within async contexts so you don't need to manually manage thread pools or blocking operations. However, if you want true lock-free communication without any synchronization overhead at all, then channels become much more interesting.
So tokio::sync::Mutex still has some cost even though it's async-aware? What about mpsc channels? Do they really solve my problem?

MPSC channels are perfect for this scenario because they allow multiple producers to send messages to a single consumer without needing any shared state or locks at all. Think of it like a postal system: instead of everyone waiting in line to drop mail into one box, each person sends their letter through the mailman who delivers it directly to your inbox.
Wait, so I can just replace my counter with an mpsc channel and let tasks push values instead of trying to update a shared integer?

Exactly. Instead of having multiple tasks try to increment a single atomic or mutex-protected variable, you create a channel where each task sends its result independently, and the receiver aggregates them later. This eliminates contention entirely.
What if I need both async execution AND shared state like a config file that multiple services read?

For that use case you might consider using watch channels or actor patterns. With the actor pattern, each service becomes an independent actor receiving messages via a channel rather than trying to mutate shared memory directly.
So I shouldn't be thinking about std::sync::Mutex in async code at all unless absolutely necessary?

Correct. In modern Rust async development, tokio::sync::Mutex is your go-to for when you do need to share state within a single thread context, but for cross-task coordination, prefer channels or watch streams.
One last thing: what happens if I accidentally use std::sync::Mutex inside an async block without the proper wrapper?

You'll hit that exact error you mentioned earlier because std::sync::Mutex relies on thread-local storage that doesn't exist in async contexts. The tokio runtime will panic before your code even runs when it tries to acquire the lock.

I just got my first job and I'm trying to port a legacy system over. I have some code that uses a std::sync::Mutex around shared data, but now I need to make it async. Here is the snippet I'm stuck on: let mut handle = tokio::spawn(async move { loop { let guard = state.lock().await; } });

You are encountering a common mistake where developers try to apply synchronous locking patterns directly into an async context without understanding the underlying mechanics. The issue with your snippet is that std::sync::Mutex is not compatible with tokio's runtime because it relies on blocking threads, which can stall the entire event loop if called from within an async block. You should be using tokio::sync::Mutex instead, but even then, you cannot use await inside a loop like this without creating new tasks for each iteration.
Ah okay so I should just swap std::sync::Mutex with tokio::sync::Mutex and keep the await? But my code still looks weird. It seems like I'm trying to lock something while already inside a spawn task.

You are right that you should swap std::sync::Mutex with tokio::sync::Mutex, but the real problem is architectural. You are thinking about concurrency as if it were single-threaded blocking calls. In async Rust, a tokio::sync::Mutex allows multiple tasks to contend for the lock using cooperative multitasking, but your loop structure implies you want to run this repeatedly in one task indefinitely.
So I can't just use a mutex here? Because if I do, it will block my async runtime?

You are correct that using std::sync::Mutex in an async context would block the runtime, but even tokio::sync::Mutex has limitations when used inside a single task loop without care. The deeper issue is that you are trying to maintain shared state from within a spawned task while expecting it to be accessible globally or repeatedly locked without spawning new tasks each time.
I think I understand the blocking part, but what about the shared state? How do other parts of my app access this data?

You are missing the key insight here: in an actor model or channel-based design, you should not share mutable state directly between tasks. Instead, consider using tokio::sync::mpsc channels to send messages representing changes to your data, or use a watch channel pattern where actors publish events instead of trying to lock global variables.
But I need the actual data structure. If I don't share state directly, how do I know what changed?

You are thinking about it like a synchronous database query. In async Rust, you should rely on senders and receivers to communicate changes rather than trying to maintain a single mutable object accessible by all tasks. Consider implementing an actor pattern where your task publishes events via mpsc channels or uses tokio::sync::watch channels to notify others of state updates.
Okay so I should probably refactor my code to use a channel instead of trying to lock the same data? That sounds like what I was missing all along.

You have hit on it. The common mistake is treating async concurrency as just synchronous concurrency with different locks. You should refactor your code to use tokio::sync::mpsc channels or watch channels so that each task communicates changes through messages rather than trying to lock shared memory.
Wait, I think I see it now. Instead of locking state, I should be sending updates via a channel and reacting to them in my loop. That's the actor pattern without needing mutexes at all!

Exactly. You have achieved an aha moment: by using tokio::sync::mpsc channels or watch channels, you can communicate state changes without any explicit locking, which is the core benefit of async Rust design.
